title: Mushroom bhaji
subtitle: Dry-spiced mushrooms with cumin and green chilli
excerpt: Mushrooms cooked quickly in spiced oil with cumin seeds, turmeric, garlic, and green chilli until their moisture has cooked off and the spice coating has dried onto their surface. A dry side dish with no sauce, ordered as a vegetarian accompaniment at the curry-house table. The mushrooms absorb the spices intensely while shrinking to a concentrated, earthy result.
sourceNotes: Mushroom bhaji is a standard vegetarian side dish on British Indian menus. 'Bhaji' in this context means a dry vegetable dish (not to be confused with onion bhajis, which are fritters). The dish uses the same dry-spicing technique applied to saag aloo and aloo gobi.

content.text: Mushrooms release a large quantity of moisture when they hit a hot pan. For bhaji, this moisture must be cooked off completely before the dish is considered done — otherwise the result is a wet, soupy side dish rather than a dry-spiced one. Use high heat, don't cover the pan, and stir regularly. The mushrooms will shrink dramatically and the liquid that pools in the pan will evaporate; the dish is ready when the pan is almost dry and the mushrooms have a glazed, spice-coated surface.

Button mushrooms or chestnut mushrooms both work well. Larger mushrooms can be quartered; button mushrooms can be left whole or halved. The size affects cooking time — smaller pieces cook and release moisture faster. Don't mix different sizes in the same batch.

items.prepNote: halved or quartered depending on size

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over high heat. Add the {{cumin-seeds}} and fry for 20 seconds. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-green}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} and increase the heat to maximum. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring regularly, until all the moisture released by the mushrooms has evaporated and the pan is almost dry. The mushrooms should look glazed and the spices should be coating them rather than pooling in liquid. Season with salt, stir in the {{garam-masala}}, and scatter with coriander if using.

items.symptom: Wet and soupy rather than dry
items.cause: Pan lid on or heat too low
items.fix: Cook uncovered on high heat throughout. Stir regularly to prevent sticking but allow the moisture to evaporate
Once all the liquid has evaporated, reduce the heat slightly and stir more frequently. The mushrooms are more prone to sticking once dry

items.symptom: No spice flavour on the mushrooms
items.cause: Spices added after mushroom liquid pooled, diluting them
items.fix: Add the spices before the mushrooms, cooking them in the dry pan for 30 seconds first. The mushroom moisture will then dissolve the spices into the coating
500 g of raw mushrooms reduces to roughly 200 g when dry-cooked. The concentrated flavour is the point; this is a side dish, not a main

content.text: Mushroom bhaji is the simplest dry-spiced side on the British Indian menu and among the quickest to make. It is often ordered as a filler alongside a main curry and rice, providing a textural and flavour contrast without the preparation complexity of saag aloo or aloo gobi. The earthiness of the mushrooms absorbs the cumin and turmeric well — a combination that appears in Indian cooking far less frequently than mushrooms in Western cooking, but with equally successful results.
